text: The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt and mobilizes the right colon medially. The duodenum is kocherized to expose the right renal hilum, and the tissue over the inferior vena cava is excised.

The surgeon highlights the gonadal vein with a green arrow and retracts the ureter superiorly. The psoas muscle is visible in the surgical field. Dissection continues along the anterior aspect of the inferior vena cava, revealing the renal vein. The renal artery is clamped and divided using a laparoscopic vascular stapler.

An ultrasound probe is used to delineate the extent of the renal vein thrombus, which reaches up to the vena cava. A suture is placed around the renal vein to facilitate the milking of the thrombus up the vein. A clamp is positioned below the suture, and a stick tie is applied to secure the renal vein, providing venous control with both the clip and suture visible.

A second clip is placed on the renal vein, which is then opened to visualize the lumen and confirm the absence of thrombus. The lumen appears clean. Dissection proceeds along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava (IVC), dividing the overlying tissue to reach the adrenal vein level. The adrenal gland is carefully separated from the vena cava.

The surgeon clips and divides the adrenal vein using a harmonic device. Superior dissection is performed to mobilize the kidney, with lateral tissue division aiding in complete mobilization. The kidney specimen is placed in a fifteen millimeter endokitch bag for later retrieval. Lymph node dissection is conducted, and the vena cava is fully mobilized from surrounding lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon highlights the renal vein on the left side and proceeds to divide the lymphatic package, part of the pericaval lymph nodes. A nodal packet is fully mobilized and placed in a ten millimeter endo-catch bag. Additional peri-caval and retro-caval lymph nodes are mobilized, with lymphatic tissue being divided after clipping.

The surgeon dissects the pericable lymph node around the renal hilum, removing bulky lymphatic tissue. The dissection follows a template similar to that used for testicular RPLND, ensuring all visible tissue seen on the CT scan is excised.

The surgeon mobilizes lymph node packets and places them in a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag for later delivery. The left renal vein is exposed, and the aorta is highlighted with a green arrow as surrounding tissue is carefully exposed.

The right renal artery is exposed in the interaerial cable space, with the IVC being retracted superiorly. A bulky retrocaval lymph node is highlighted and dissected off the right renal artery. The renal artery is visibly pulsating. A Kittener is used to retract the vena cava, facilitating access to the large lymph node, which is then removed and placed in an endocatch bag.

The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, carefully avoiding injury to the common iliac vessels as the aorta and vena cava bifurcate. The inferior-most lymph node, visible on the CT scan, is dissected, with lymphatic tissue clipped before cutting to prevent chyle and lymph leaks.

The surgeon examines the pericaval space and the interaorta cable space, where the aorta and right renal artery are visible. Dense adhesions of lymphatic tissue to the vena cava are addressed using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, while the vena cava is superiorly retracted. No nodal tissues remain in the dissected area.
